title: Lesson 298
transcript: Okay, Rob, we are in domain number seven. Yeah. And let me just explain what domain number seven, first of all, is all about. As you know, it's called security operations. What that means is the day-to-day activities that the security function participates in, supports. initiates those day-to-day things that any security function and the people that work in that security function are going to participate in on a regular basis. The way that this domain is structured is in modules again that talk about what security really needs to participate in and support, initiate. and so on. I think we're going to start with there's a bit of a flow problem here but again our modules I think are based on the exam outline so You know, we're going to start off with something that you're really passionate about and an expert on, and that is supporting criminal investigations. Supporting or conducting or participating? Yeah, sure. All of that. Yeah. So yeah, this first section here is about how do you actually conduct a forensic investigation? And so to start to explain this, I'd like to set up a scenario for you. But before I do that, I wanna point out something important, and that is, if you're gonna be conducting a forensic investigation, especially around computer systems and computer forensics, you need to know what you're doing, because I think there's a technical term for it. If you don't know what you're doing, Screw it up. So you need to know what you're doing. You need to have the right training because as we'll explain, collecting some of this digital forensic evidence is difficult. If you do it wrong, you're gonna mess it up. So here's the scene, okay? Here's what I'd like you to create a mental image of. So you've somehow detected that someone has broken into your corporate environment and you've traced the attack back to their criminal lair. So as you descend the stairs into their parents' basement. Of course they live in their parents' basement. That's Rob. Hey, hey. I live in the upstairs, thank you. As you just stand in the stairs of the parents' basement and you see that everything's sort of Cheeto-dusted and smells of despair, you've arrived at the crime scene. So what's the first thing you're gonna do, John? mess up the crime scene, contaminate the evidence. Yeah. No, I mean, we need to conduct a reliable investigation, right? We have to be following proper standards. We need to know what we're doing. Because there's a slide here, right? Securing the scene is the key word, right? That's the key thing that we want to do. What does securing the scene mean, John? not contaminate the evidence. You need to block it off and start doing things in the right order based on skills and having the right tools. Yeah, and anyone that's watched, what's that show? There you go, CSI Miami or whatever. If you ever watch that show, you'll notice one of the first things that they do when they arrive on a crime scene is they take a bunch of photographs. It's part of securing the scene, making sure you keep people from traipsing through your scene, but also take photographs. Start to establish what was there when you arrived. And it's the same idea even when computer forensics start to capture log files, start to capture perhaps VM snapshot, things like that. But it's all about securing the scene and not contaminating the evidence. Yeah, I think the whole important point from a court perspective is that you need to show the integrity of the scene before you start looking for evidence. That's a requirement and I think the point we're trying to make here is that it's much easier to do in a physical crime scene than it is in a cyber or technology scene, but the same requirement exists in a court of law that you have to show the integrity of that scene. Absolutely. And I love the statement, you know, once you've contaminated evidence, it can't be decontaminated. So that's why it's so critical to start with that. So I think we have a quick overview of the forensic investigation process here. You know, we've never heard that there's exact questions on this process, but I think it's a good idea to have an understanding of the sort of major steps that we're going through. So we start with identification. What's that about? Well, that's the identification of the crime scene itself, blocking it off and, you know, again, showing that integrity of the scene itself, yeah. And then you start with preservation. That's obviously preserving any of the evidence that you're finding there. And then you move into actual collection. That's kind of the tag, bag, and carry type stuff we'll talk about in a minute. And then you move into examination. This is where you're actually examining the evidence. Looking for? What did someone do, I guess, right? Well, and you're going to talk about this. I think mom is what you're going to address. You've probably heard that from crime shows. Mom, motive, opportunity, means. Talk about that. And then you go into analysis, presentation to whomever you're going to present this to eventually. And then this is an important point. When we're talking about investigations here, we're not just talking about those that might end up in a court of law. We can also be talking about administrative investigations conducted just within a company or regulatory investigations. We'll talk about that in a couple modules from now. And then the final piece is decision. The whole point of doing these investigations is so someone can decide, was this a crime or not? Did someone do something untoward within your organization? So that's kind of the point of all this. All right, so then we move into the evidence actual collection. So when we're collecting evidence, what are we doing? Yeah, so if you watch crime shows, they always talk about this. And what's important is to maintain what's called a chain of custody. And that's definitely something that we need to keep an eye on. You'll definitely see it. But the chain of custody is all about the word control. from the time that you collect it to the time that you present it in court you need to ensure and prove to the court that it was always in your control which means there is no opportunity for it to be changed or tampered with or even looked at by unauthorized individuals that's why Probably when you watch these crime shows someone is always identified as being in charge of the evidence and what they do is they Well, you're gonna talk about bag tagging and all that kind of stuff. There's the next slide, right? They they tag bag and carry it not terminology. You'll probably see in the exam, but it's that concept of When you do find a piece of evidence, a log file, a piece of malware, whatever, it's this idea that you very carefully identify it, protect it, and have established the chain of custody, which means you have control over the evidence from the moment it's collected. Maybe a good time to say the chain of custody does not ensure admissibility, but it simply proves control from the time that you collect it to the time that you present it in court, which could be months or years in the future. Yeah. So what are some sources of information evidence? Where can we get this evidence from? Yeah, these are just examples that you see here. I mean, obviously evidence can be gotten from people orally, written statements like it says here, documents or maybe contracts, that sort of thing. And of course, anything that is produced by a computer or technology, log files. and possibly some other things that can reconstruct what that event was that happened, visual, audio, anything that you see on here. One of the, there's this dude called Locard. He was kind of like the French version of Sherlock Holmes. And he came up with this, it's called the Locard's Exchange Principle. And it's something that you can use when you're conducting investigation to help you identify evidence. Because Locard basically said, whenever anyone perpetrates a crime, they're gonna leave something behind and they're gonna take something with them which is why in the crime shows you always see them like carefully vacuuming the carpet at the crime scene looking for that one hair or you know they're looking at the mud and the tires of the car to see what sort of evidence was taken with the car when they were at the crime scene so you can use low cards principle to help you figure out where you could look for evidence yeah And that's the whole purpose of investigations, as you said, to look for whatever was left at the scene or taken to potentially identify suspects that may have committed the crime. And the last piece we'll talk about here is you always want to be looking for mum, right? Motive, opportunity, means. How do we use mum when we're conducting an investigation? Well, if any one of those are missing, it means that that person or that suspect could not have committed the crime. And so as an investigator, I think the objective is to find proof based on mom, but also to disprove that someone may have committed the crime. There you go. There's an introduction to investigations and we'll continue in the next module.
